aliases: Indian cottage cheese dumplings in cream

english: Indian cottage cheese dumplings in cream

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Ras Malai. FOODON_03302050 (cottage cheese food product) is used as the closest parent, as Ras Malai is made from chenna (fresh cottage cheese) poached and soaked in sweetened reduced milk.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FMT (milk-based dessert) is the closest defensible parent category. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for Ras Malai.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Ras Malai or a sufficiently close parent concept in the clinical food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Ras Malai consists of 2 soft chenna dumplings soaked in sweetened reduced milk (rabri), weighing approximately 100 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Ras Malai (milk-based sweet)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) does not list Ras Malai as a standalone entry. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for chenna-based sweets and rabri (reduced sweetened milk), weighted by typical recipe proportions: approximately 50% chenna dumplings and 50% sweetened reduced milk. Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Indian milk-based desserts to validate macro plausibility. Energy calculated from macros using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — milk-based desserts and dairy sweets
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Ras Malai exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 47 is estimated by analogy with similar milk-based Indian sweets (kheer / rice pudding with milk GI ~47, and dairy-based desserts with high milk content). The high milk and chenna protein content suppresses the glycemic response relative to the sugar content. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (47 × 23.5) / 100 ≈ 11.0 per 100 g serving. This is a conservative analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~55 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Ras Malai contains a relatively low sodium level of approximately 55 mg per 100 g, making it acceptable in moderate portions for individuals managing hypertension, though the high sugar content warrants attention to overall dietary balance.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (~21 g per 100 g) despite low GI (~47)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although Ras Malai has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 47 due to its high milk protein and fat content, the substantial sugar load of ~21 g per 100 g means that portion control is essential for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, and consumption should be limited to small servings on an occasional basis.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.9 g per 100 g) and high sugar content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ras Malai contains moderate saturated fat (~4.9 g per 100 g) from full-fat milk and chenna, along with high added sugar (~21 g per 100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption to reduce saturated fat and sugar intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ras Malai (also spelled Rasmalai or Rossomalai) is a classic North and East Indian milk-based dessert (mithai) with origins in Bengal. It consists of soft, spongy dumplings made from chenna (freshly curdled cottage cheese, similar to paneer), which are gently poached until light and airy, then soaked in a rich, sweetened, reduced milk syrup (rabri) flavoured with cardamom, saffron, and rose water. The texture is delicate and melt-in-the-mouth, with a mildly sweet, creamy, and floral flavour profile. Ras Malai is widely served at Indian festive occasions, weddings, and as a restaurant dessert across South Asia and the Indian diaspora globally, including Singapore. Nutritionally, it provides approximately 186 kcal per 100 g, with 6.5 g protein from dairy, 7.8 g fat (predominantly saturated from milk), and 23.5 g carbohydrate of which ~21 g is sugar. It is a good source of calcium (~195 mg per 100 g) and vitamin B12 (~0.4 mcg per 100 g) from its high dairy content. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 47 (low), moderated by the protein and fat matrix of milk and chenna. Despite the low GI, the high sugar content means individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume it in small portions. It is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian, but contains dairy and is not suitable for vegans or those with lactose intolerance.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Ras Malai?
What is the GI of Ras Malai?
Is Ras Malai high in sugar?

confidence_reason: ICMR-NIN does not publish a standalone Ras Malai entry; nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chenna and rabri component data and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy dairy dessert entries. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using comparable milk-based Indian sweets. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides a direct Ras Malai entry with all macros and GI measured.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN (2017) component data for chenna and rabri, weighted by typical recipe proportions. Direct measurement of a standardised Ras Malai recipe is recommended to upgrade confidence to high. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): Ras Malai contains no plant fibre-bearing ingredients; chenna and sweetened milk are the sole components, and fibre is confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN dairy food data. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 6. GI of 47 is an analogous estimate based on comparable milk-based Indian sweets from the University of Sydney GI Database; direct GI measurement is recommended. 7. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (47 × 23.5) / 100 = 11.0 for a 100 g serving. 8. The restaurant entry from field survey data (Indian House/Dumpukht Grills & Curry - Boat Quay) describes this as a chef's special mini version; nutrient values reflect a standard Ras Malai recipe and may vary by restaurant preparation.
